file_name: GentechHoldingsInc_20190808_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_11776814_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_Distributor Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: (b) Prices and Shipment. Company agrees to sell, and Distributor agrees to purchase, the Products in accordance with the Prices and Volume Discount Prices set forth in Schedule A. Such Prices and Volume Discount Prices shall only be subject to increase once per year on each anniversary date of this Agreement, provided (i) Company provides Distributor with at least Ninety (90) days prior written notice of any such increase, and (ii) such increase does not exceed 5% of the preceding year's Prices, except for reasons of force majeure, (Chapter 10), and Volume Discount Prices nor the lowest price charged to others for the same Product. Any such change shall not apply for any order(s) accepted prior to the effective date of the increase.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Prices and Shipment. Company agrees to sell, and Distributor agrees to purchase, the Products in accordance with the Prices and Volume Discount Prices set forth in Schedule A. Such Prices and Volume Discount Prices may be subject to increase at any time without prior notice.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior notice and the limitation on price increases introduces a legal contradiction. Many states have laws requiring reasonable notice for price increases, especially in ongoing commercial relationships. This omission makes the contract unfavorable and potentially illegal to the distributor, who is not protected against arbitrary price changes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-305. Open Price Term
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-305 states that if a price is left to be fixed by the seller, it means a price for him or her to fix in good faith. By removing the prior notice and price increase limits, the contract potentially violates the UCC's requirement of good faith in setting prices, as it allows the company to increase prices arbitrarily, and thus potentially violating that requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires a 90-day notice for price increases and limits the increase to 5%, except in cases of force majeure. The changed text removes these consumer protections entirely, allowing price increases at any time without notice. UCC § 2-305(2) states that a price to be fixed by the seller means a price for him to fix in good faith. The removal of the notice and price limits allows the company to arbitrarily increase prices, potentially violating the UCC's requirement of good faith. The omission of these protections creates a contract less favorable to the distributor, making it potentially non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Company warrants and represents that the Products will be free from defects in design, materials and workmanship and conform with any specifications provided.
perturbation.changed_text: Company makes no warranties regarding the Products.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the warranty clause eliminates the implied warranty of merchantability, which is a standard consumer protection. This violates the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), which implies certain warranties unless explicitly disclaimed in a conspicuous manner.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 implies a warranty that goods shall be merchantable if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. By stating that the Company makes no warranties, this directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability unless it is conspicuously disclaimed, which it is not in this modified section.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the warranty entirely, while the original text provides a warranty on the products. UCC § 2-314, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind' unless excluded or modified per section 2-316. The new text contradicts this implied warranty by making 'no warranties regarding the Products.'

perturbation.original_text: The Parties will share equally the cost of arbitrating such dispute. The arbitrator(s) shall not be empowered to award punitive or other damages in excess of compensatory damages, and both parties irrevocably waive the right to any such damages. Judgment on the award rendered by the arbitrator(s) may be entered by any court having jurisdiction over the dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will each bear their own costs associated with arbitrating such dispute. The arbitrator(s) shall not be empowered to award punitive or other damages in excess of compensatory damages, and both parties irrevocably waive the right to any such damages. Judgment on the award rendered by the arbitrator(s) may be entered by any court having jurisdiction over the dispute.
perturbation.explanation: This change, where each party bears their own cost for arbitration instead of splitting the costs, introduces a legal risk. In some jurisdictions, particularly in disputes involving smaller businesses or consumers, such a clause could be seen as unconscionable or an attempt to deter legitimate claims, because it would place an unduly high cost burden on one of the parties to resolve the dispute.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct contradiction, this alteration introduces the possibility of running afoul of Florida Statutes § 682.11, which addresses costs and fees in arbitration. Depending on the nature of the dispute and the specific costs incurred, a court could find that requiring each party to bear their own costs, particularly if one party is significantly disadvantaged, is an unfair or unconscionable application of the arbitration agreement, which goes against the state's policies regarding fair arbitration procedures.

F.S. 682.11 682.11 Remedies; fees and expenses of arbitration proceeding. — (1) An arbitrator may award punitive damages or other exemplary relief if such an award is authorized by law in a civil action involving the same claim and the evidence produced at the hearing justifies the award under the legal standards otherwise applicable to the claim. (2) An arbitrator may award reasonable attorney fees and other reasonable expenses of arbitration if such an award is authorized by law in a civil action involving the same claim or by the agreement of the parties to the arbitration proceeding. (3) As to all remedies other than those authorized by subsections (1) and (2), an arbitrator may order such remedies as the arbitrator considers just and appropriate under the circumstances of the arbitration proceeding. The fact that such a remedy could not or would not be granted by the court is not a ground for refusing to confirm an award under s. 682.12 or for vacating an award under s. 682.13 . (4) An arbitrator’s expenses and fees, together with other expenses, must be paid as provided in the award. (5) If an arbitrator awards punitive damages or other exemplary relief under subsection (1), the arbitrator shall specify in the award the basis in fact justifying and the basis in law authorizing the award and state separately the amount of the punitive damages or other exemplary relief. History. — s. 10, ch. 57-402; s. 12, ch. 67-254; s. 22, ch. 2013-232. Note. — Former s. 57.20.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original agreement regarding the cost of arbitration. Instead of the parties sharing the cost equally, each party bears their own costs. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this could be viewed as unconscionable, especially if one party is at a disadvantage. Florida Statute 682.11(4) states that 'An arbitrator’s expenses and fees, together with other expenses, must be paid as provided in the award.' While the statute itself does not explicitly mandate an equal split, the alteration could lead to unfair arbitration proceedings, contravening the state's policies. Therefore, the modification introduces a legal contradiction, particularly in scenarios where it places an undue burden on one party, potentially deterring legitimate claims.
